description: The side plank with rotation combines isometric core stability with dynamic rotational movement, creating an effective HIIT exercise that builds lateral and rotational core strength while elevating heart rate through continuous controlled movement.
equipment: Non-slip Surface, Exercise Mat (recommended)
form_tips: Maintain straight line from head to heels in side plank; Stack feet for standard difficulty (stagger for easier variation); Keep supporting shoulder directly above elbow; Control rotational movement; Breathe rhythmically throughout; Engage core continuously; Focus on quality of movement over speed; Maintain neutral neck position.
instructions:
1. Begin in side plank position on right forearm, body forming straight line from head to heels.
2. Place left hand behind head, elbow pointing toward ceiling.
3. Engage core and ensure body is properly aligned before beginning movement.
4. Rotate torso by reaching left elbow down toward floor/mat, moving under ribcage.
5. Control movement with core muscles, avoiding momentum.
6. Rotate back through starting position and extend left arm toward ceiling.
7. Complete prescribed repetitions on right side, then switch to left side.
8. Maintain consistent rhythm and breathing pattern throughout set.
name: Side Plank with Rotation
variations: Side Plank with Thread the Needle; Side Plank Rotation with Extended Arm; Side Plank Rotation with Weighted Hand; Elevated Side Plank Rotation (feet on bench); Dynamic Side Plank Rotation (faster tempo); Side Plank Rotation with Leg Lift

common_mistakes: Poor hand positioning; Excessive bouncing; Limited hip mobility; Not engaging core; Poor foot placement; Rushing transitions; Using momentum rather than control.
description: The dumbbell walkout burpee is a compound movement that combines horizontal loading with explosive full-body conditioning, creating a challenging HIIT exercise that builds core stability, upper body strength, and cardiovascular endurance simultaneously.
equipment: Dumbbells (Pair, Light to Moderate Weight), Non-slip Surface
form_tips: Maintain neutral spine during walkout phase; Keep core engaged throughout entire movement; Control descent to floor; Avoid excessive lower back arching; Place hands directly under shoulders during plank; Jump with control, landing softly; Breathe rhythmically despite high exertion; Maintain consistent pace without sacrificing form.
instructions:
1. Begin standing with feet hip-width apart, holding dumbbells at sides.
2. Hinge at hips and place dumbbells on floor shoulder-width apart.
3. Keeping legs as straight as possible (slight knee bend allowed), walk hands forward on dumbbells into plank position.
4. Ensure body forms straight line from head to heels in plank.
5. Perform one push-up (optional for increased difficulty).
6. Walk hands back toward feet, maintaining control and core tension.
7. As you return to standing position, drive through heels and jump vertically.
8. Land softly with knees slightly bent, immediately beginning next repetition.
name: Dumbbell Walkout Burpee
variations: Dumbbell Walkout to Renegade Row; Walkout Burpee without Jump; Single-Arm Dumbbell Walkout; Elevated Dumbbell Walkout (feet on bench); Dumbbell Walkout to Cross-Body Mountain Climber; Dumbbell Walkout Burpee Complex

common_mistakes: Limited range of motion; Poor posture; Relying on arms rather than legs; Holding breath; Insufficient core engagement; Looking down; Excessive vertical movement (bobbing).
description: The speed skater is a lateral plyometric exercise that simulates the movement pattern of speed skating, creating a high-intensity, low-impact cardio exercise that builds lateral power, agility, and cardiovascular endurance while engaging multiple muscle groups.
equipment: Non-slip Surface, Optional: Small Cones/Markers
form_tips: Drive with outside leg for power; Maintain athletic posture throughout; Land softly with bent knee on leading leg; Allow trailing leg to extend naturally; Touch floor during crossover for balance if needed; Keep gaze forward and chest up; Maintain rhythm despite fatigue; Use arms for balance and additional momentum.
instructions:
1. Begin standing with feet together or hip-width apart in athletic stance.
2. Push off right foot and leap laterally to the left, landing softly on left foot.
3. As you land, allow right leg to naturally cross behind left leg, extending to side.
4. Touch right hand or fingertips to floor for balance if needed (optional).
5. Maintain athletic position with chest up and hips back throughout movement.
6. Without pausing, immediately push off left foot to leap to the right side.
7. Land softly on right foot, allowing left leg to cross behind.
8. Continue alternating side-to-side leaps in fluid, rhythmic motion for prescribed time or repetitions.
name: Speed Skater
variations: Speed Skater with Floor Touch; Weighted Speed Skater (holding light dumbbells); Speed Skater with Hop; Box Speed Skater (jumping over obstacle); Extended Range Speed Skater (greater lateral distance); Speed Skater with Hold (pause at each side)

common_mistakes: Using momentum; Poor slam technique; Limited hip drive; Bouncing ball too much; Incomplete hip extension; Not maintaining athletic stance; Inconsistent rhythm; Poor breathing coordination.
description: The slam ball circuit combines various dynamic movements using a slam ball into a structured interval format, creating a comprehensive HIIT workout that develops power, core strength, and cardiovascular endurance through explosive full-body movements.
equipment: Slam Ball (15-30 lbs), Non-slip Surface, Timer, Optional: Cones/Markers
form_tips: Generate power from hips and legs, not just arms; Maintain athletic stance between exercises; Use full body coordination during movements; Control ball during all phases; Execute exercises with proper form despite fatigue; Breathe rhythmically with movement patterns; Transition efficiently between exercises; Scale weight appropriately to maintain intensity.
instructions:
1. Select 4-6 complementary slam ball exercises (e.g., overhead slams, rotational slams, chest passes, squat throws).
2. Arrange exercises in circuit format with minimal transition time.
3. Begin with thorough warm-up focusing on shoulders, hips, and core.
4. Perform first exercise at high intensity for prescribed time (typically 30-45 seconds).
5. Transition immediately to next exercise or rest briefly (10-15 seconds) if needed.
6. Complete all selected exercises in sequence to complete one circuit.
7. Rest 1-2 minutes between full circuits if performing multiple rounds.
8. Focus on maintaining power output and proper form throughout entire workout.
name: Slam Ball Circuit
variations: Tabata Slam Ball Workout; EMOM Slam Ball Circuit; Heavy-Light Contrast Circuit; Partner Slam Ball Circuit; Slam Ball and Bodyweight Complex; Progressive Slam Ball Workout (increasing difficulty); Slam Ball Density Training

common_mistakes: Excessive arm movement; Incomplete jack range; Limited lunge depth; Poor lunge knee alignment; Looking down; Landing heavily; Rushing transitions; Inconsistent breathing pattern.
description: The jumping jack to reverse lunge combines a high-intensity cardiovascular movement with a lower-body strength and balance challenge, creating an effective HIIT exercise that elevates heart rate while building coordination, agility, and muscular endurance.
equipment: Non-slip Surface, Optional: Exercise Mat
form_tips: Land softly during jumping jacks; Maintain upright posture throughout; Control lunge descent; Keep front knee aligned with toes during lunge; Engage core during entire movement; Breathe rhythmically with movement pattern; Step back far enough for proper lunge alignment; Drive through heel of front foot when rising from lunge.
instructions:
1. Begin standing with feet together, arms at sides.
2. Perform standard jumping jack: jump feet out wider than shoulder-width while raising arms overhead.
3. Jump feet back together while lowering arms to sides.
4. Complete designated number of jumping jacks (typically 2-4).
5. From standing position, step backward with right leg into reverse lunge position.
6. Lower body until both knees form approximately 90-degree angles.
7. Push through left heel to return to standing position.
8. Repeat sequence, alternating lunge leg each time after completing jumping jacks.
name: Jumping Jack to Reverse Lunge
variations: Jumping Jack to Curtsy Lunge; Jumping Jack to Forward Lunge; Jumping Jack to Lateral Lunge; Seal Jack to Reverse Lunge; Jumping Jack to Lunge with Rotation; Jumping Jack to Lunge Jump

common_mistakes: Limited squat depth; Poor foot placement; Incorrect knee alignment; Chest dropping forward; Losing balance during box step-up; Using momentum; Poor breathing coordination; Inconsistent repetition tempo.
description: The dumbbell squat to box step-up combines two lower-body strength movements into one comprehensive exercise that elevates heart rate while building functional strength in multiple planes of motion, making it ideal for HIIT circuits focused on lower body development.
equipment: Dumbbells (Pair), Sturdy Box/Bench (12-24 inches), Non-slip Surface
form_tips: Maintain upright torso during squat; Keep weight in heels during squat; Place entire foot on box for step-up; Drive through heel of stepping foot; Avoid pushing off back foot during step-up; Maintain neutral spine throughout; Control descent from box; Breathe rhythmically with movement (exhale during exertion).
instructions:
1. Begin standing with feet shoulder-width apart, holding dumbbells at sides, facing box/bench.
2. Initiate squat by hinging hips back and bending knees.
3. Descend until thighs are parallel to ground or slightly below.
4. Drive through heels to return to standing position.
5. Immediately step right foot completely onto box.
6. Drive through right heel to bring left foot up to box, standing fully upright.
7. Step down with left foot first, then right foot.
8. Repeat sequence, alternating leading leg on box step-up after each squat.
name: Dumbbell Squat to Box Step-Up
variations: Goblet Squat to Step-Up; Front Rack Squat to Step-Up; Squat to Lateral Step-Up; Squat to High Step-Up (increasing box height); Squat to Box Step-Up with Knee Drive; Squat to Alternating Step-Up with Shoulder Press

description: The high knee sprint in place is a high-intensity, low-impact cardio exercise that simulates sprinting mechanics without forward travel, creating an effective HIIT movement that develops running form, cardiovascular fitness, and power output in minimal space.
equipment: Non-slip Surface, Optional: Timer, Mirror (for form check)
form_tips: Drive knees to hip height or higher; Maintain upright posture (slight forward lean acceptable); Land on balls of feet; Coordinate reciprocal arm action with leg drive; Maintain consistent effort despite fatigue; Focus on quick ground contacts; Breathe rhythmically with movement; Keep shoulders relaxed and down.
instructions:
1. Begin standing tall with feet hip-width apart, arms at sides.
2. Engage core and adopt athletic stance with slight bend in knees.
3. Drive right knee upward toward chest while simultaneously driving left arm forward and right arm back.
4. Land lightly on ball of right foot as you immediately drive left knee upward.
5. Continue alternating knees at rapid pace, focusing on height and quick turnover.
6. Maintain coordinated arm swing throughout, driving elbows back and forth.
7. Focus on lifting knees to hip height or slightly higher for maximum effectiveness.
8. Continue at maximum sustainable intensity for prescribed time interval (typically 20-45 seconds).
name: High Knee Sprint in Place
variations: High Knee March (slower pace); Progressive High Knees (increasing pace); High Knee Hold (pausing at top); High Knee A-Skip (adding hop); Weighted High Knees (ankle weights/vest); High Knee Sprint to Regular Sprint

common_mistakes: Losing cable tension; Poor spine alignment; Incomplete hip extension; Improper breathing; Knees caving inward; Rushing concentric phase; Losing core engagement; Lifting heels during squat.
description: The cable squat press is a compound movement that combines lower body strength with upper body pushing in a dynamic pattern, creating an effective HIIT exercise that builds full-body coordination, power, and cardiovascular fitness using machine resistance.
equipment: Cable Machine with Handle Attachments, Appropriate Weight Selection
form_tips: Maintain cable tension throughout movement; Keep neutral spine during entire exercise; Drive knees outward during squat; Generate pressing power primarily from legs; Fully extend hips at top of movement; Control weight during all phases; Breathe rhythmically with movement (exhale during press); Maintain core engagement throughout exercise.
instructions:
1. Position yourself facing away from cable machine, grasping handles at shoulder height.
2. Step forward to create tension in cables, feet shoulder-width apart.
3. Initiate movement by hinging hips back and bending knees into squat position.
4. Maintain upright torso and keep elbows close to body during descent.
5. Descend until thighs are parallel to ground or slightly below.
6. Drive through heels to rise, simultaneously pressing handles forward and upward.
7. Fully extend hips, knees and arms at top of movement.
8. Control return to starting position, maintaining cable tension throughout.
name: Cable Squat Press
variations: Cable Squat to Single-Arm Press; Cable Squat Press with Rotation; Alternating Cable Squat Press; Cable Squat with Overhead Press; Cable Squat Jump Press; Cable Split Squat Press

common_mistakes: Inconsistent tension on ropes; Poor grip; Limited hip drive; Hunching shoulders; Rigid knees; Losing rhythm; Using too much upper body, not enough lower body; Improper breathing technique.
description: The battle rope jumping jack combines the upper body conditioning of battle ropes with the cardiovascular benefits of jumping jacks, creating a full-body HIIT exercise that develops shoulder endurance, coordination, and metabolic conditioning.
equipment: Battle Ropes (30-50 ft length), Anchor Point, Non-slip Surface
form_tips: Maintain athletic stance with soft knees; Generate power from legs and core; Keep shoulders down and back; Maintain tension in ropes at all times; Coordinate jumping motion with arm movement; Breathe rhythmically despite high exertion; Focus on fluid, continuous movement; Minimize excessive upper body compensation.
instructions:
1. Begin standing with feet together, holding one end of rope in each hand.
2. Position yourself far enough from anchor point to maintain slight tension in ropes.
3. Adopt athletic stance with soft bend in knees, core engaged.
4. Simultaneously jump feet outward (wider than shoulder-width) while raising arms outward and upward.
5. Create wave motion in ropes by maintaining tension throughout movement.
6. Immediately jump feet back together while bringing arms downward and inward.
7. Continue movement in rhythmic fashion, coordinating upper and lower body.
8. Maintain consistent pace and rope tension for prescribed time interval (typically 20-40 seconds).
name: Battle Rope Jumping Jack
variations: Battle Rope Seal Jack; Alternating Battle Rope Jack; Battle Rope Jack to Squat; Lateral Battle Rope Jack; Single-Arm Battle Rope Jack (alternating arms); Battle Rope Power Jack (explosive movement)

common_mistakes: Slow ladder movement; Poor foot placement; Losing upper body tension; Rushing through transitions; Inconsistent effort across intervals; Excessive arm movement; Losing form due to fatigue.
description: The agility ladder sprint drill combines rapid footwork patterns with explosive straight-line speed, creating an effective HIIT exercise that develops agility, coordination, and cardiovascular fitness while improving sport-specific movement skills.
equipment: Agility Ladder, Cones/Markers, Open Space (minimum 20 yards), Timer
form_tips: Focus on quick foot contacts during ladder work; Maintain athletic posture throughout; Drive arms in sprint phase; Accelerate progressively out of ladder; Focus on foot strike and turnover during sprint; Transition smoothly between phases; Maintain consistent effort across intervals; Breathe rhythmically despite high exertion.
instructions:
1. Place agility ladder on flat surface with minimum 10-15 yards of open space beyond end of ladder.
2. Position cone or marker approximately 10-15 yards from end of ladder (sprint distance).
3. Begin at start of ladder in athletic stance, knees slightly bent, weight on balls of feet.
4. Perform selected footwork pattern through entire ladder (e.g., two feet in each square, lateral shuffle, in-out steps).
5. Upon completing ladder pattern, immediately accelerate into sprint toward marker/cone.
6. Focus on powerful arm drive and proper sprinting mechanics during acceleration phase.
7. Touch cone/marker or sprint past designated endpoint, then decelerate safely.
8. Walk back to starting position, allowing appropriate recovery before next interval (typically 30-60 seconds).

common_mistakes: Limited range of motion; Poor core stability; Inadequate power output; Breaking form during fatigue; Inconsistent pacing; Inefficient transitions; Improper machine settings; Racing through recovery periods.
description: The air bike EMOM (Every Minute On the Minute) creates a structured interval format using high-output cycling with integrated upper body movement, creating an effective metabolic conditioning workout that develops cardiovascular capacity, work output, and mental toughness through precise work-to-rest ratios.
equipment: Assault Bike/Air Bike, Timer with Audible Alert, Heart Rate Monitor (optional)
form_tips: Generate power from both arms and legs simultaneously; Maintain consistent RPM during work intervals; Maximize output during short intervals; Rest completely during prescribed recovery; Breathe rhythmically despite high exertion; Pace appropriately for interval length; Monitor heart rate if possible; Scale calories/distance targets based on fitness level.
instructions:
1. Adjust air bike seat to proper height: hip height when standing beside bike.
2. Program timer for EMOM format: work intervals beginning every 60 seconds.
3. Begin with proper positioning: feet secured in pedals, hands gripping handles firmly.
4. At start of each minute, pedal at high intensity (85-95% effort) for prescribed work duration (typically 15-30 seconds).
5. Focus on generating maximum power through coordinated arm and leg action.
6. After completing work interval, rest for remainder of minute before next interval begins.
7. Start next high-intensity interval precisely at beginning of next minute.
8. Complete prescribed number of rounds (typically 10-20 minutes total), maintaining consistent power output across all intervals.
name: Air Bike EMOM
variations: Calorie-Based EMOM (fixed calorie target each minute); Pyramid EMOM (increasing/decreasing work intervals); Power-Based EMOM (wattage targets); Air Bike Death By EMOM (adding 1 calorie each minute); Air Bike EMOM with Complementary Exercise; Heart Rate Recovery EMOM

common_mistakes: Excessive lower back arch; Using arms instead of hips; Limiting side-to-side motion; Poor grip technique; Rushing transitions; Incomplete figure-8 pattern; Letting momentum control the movement; Hunching shoulders.
description: The kettlebell figure-8 is a dynamic exercise that builds coordination, grip strength, and hip mobility by passing a kettlebell in a figure-8 pattern through and around the legs, creating an effective HIIT movement that combines strength and cardiovascular conditioning.
equipment: Kettlebell (8-16kg), Non-slip Surface
form_tips: Maintain athletic stance throughout; Hinge at hips not waist; Keep core engaged during entire movement; Use controlled hand-offs between hands; Maintain neutral spine; Look forward, not down; Breathe rhythmically despite continuous movement; Grip kettlebell firmly but not tensely.
instructions:
1. Begin standing with feet wider than shoulder-width apart, kettlebell on floor between feet.
2. Squat down and grasp kettlebell handle with right hand.
3. Maintaining slight bend in knees and hips, swing kettlebell back between legs.
4. Pass kettlebell around outside of left leg, transferring to left hand behind leg.
5. Bring left hand forward between legs, completing half of figure-8 pattern.
6. Pass kettlebell around outside of right leg, transferring back to right hand.
7. Continue fluid figure-8 pattern, maintaining consistent rhythm and controlled movement.
8. Perform for prescribed time interval or repetitions, focusing on smooth transitions and proper body positioning.
name: Kettlebell Figure-8
variations: Single-Hand Figure-8 (no transfer); Weighted Figure-8 (heavier kettlebell); Figure-8 to Hold (pausing between reps); Alternating Figure-8 (changing direction); Figure-8 with Squat; Figure-8 to Swing (combining movements)

common_mistakes: Insufficient box height awareness; Landing with straight legs; Looking at feet during jump; Poor foot placement; Inadequate hip drive; Rushing between jumps; Not using arms for momentum; Improper landing technique.
description: The plyo box lateral hurdle hop is a plyometric exercise that enhances agility and lower body power by combining lateral movement with vertical jumping, creating an effective HIIT movement that builds multi-directional explosiveness and coordination.
equipment: Plyo Box (12-24 inches), Non-slip Surface, Athletic Shoes
form_tips: Use arm drive for maximum height; Land softly with bent knees; Look at box during entire movement; Generate power from hips and legs; Coordinate upper and lower body; Reset briefly between jumps if needed; Maintain upright posture throughout; Focus on quick, powerful ground contact.
instructions:
1. Position plyo box on stable, flat surface with adequate space on both sides.
2. Begin standing approximately 2 feet to one side of box in athletic stance.
3. Perform countermovement by hinging slightly at hips and bending knees.
4. Drive arms upward while explosively extending hips, knees, and ankles to jump laterally over box.
5. Land softly on opposite side of box with knees slightly bent to absorb impact.
6. Without significant pause, immediately jump back over box to starting position.
7. Continue alternating side-to-side jumps for prescribed repetitions or time.
8. Focus on height, control, and proper landing mechanics throughout exercise.
name: Plyo Box Lateral Hurdle Hop
variations: Single-Leg Lateral Hop; Weighted Vest Lateral Hop; Front-to-Back Box Hop; 180° Lateral Box Hop; Continuous Box Circuit (multiple boxes); Progressive Height Lateral Hop

common_mistakes: Using incorrect weight; Performing exercises too quickly; Poor transitions between movements; Incomplete range of motion; Inconsistent workout density; Losing form during fatigue; Improper breathing patterns; Limited rest between sets.
description: The dumbbell complex HIIT is a metabolic conditioning workout that combines multiple strength exercises performed in sequence with the same pair of dumbbells, creating a comprehensive HIIT session that builds strength endurance, cardiovascular fitness, and full-body conditioning.
equipment: Dumbbells (Pair, Moderate Weight), Non-slip Surface, Timer
form_tips: Select appropriate weight (erring on lighter side); Complete full range of motion for each exercise; Transition smoothly between movements; Maintain proper form despite fatigue; Rest completely between rounds; Breathe rhythmically throughout complex; Scale movements as needed; Prioritize technique over speed.
instructions:
1. Select 4-6 complementary dumbbell exercises (e.g., squat, push press, bent-over row, Romanian deadlift, lunge).
2. Choose appropriate weight that allows completion of all exercises with proper form.
3. Perform first exercise for prescribed repetitions (typically 5-10 per movement).
4. Transition immediately to next exercise without setting dumbbells down.
5. Complete all selected exercises in sequence with minimal transition time to finish one round.
6. Rest 60-90 seconds between rounds to allow adequate recovery.
7. Complete prescribed number of rounds (typically 3-5 total).
8. Focus on maintaining quality movement patterns despite increasing fatigue.
name: Dumbbell Complex HIIT
variations: Light Weight/High Rep Complex; Heavy Weight/Low Rep Complex; Time-Based Complex (fixed work periods); Upper Body Complex; Lower Body Complex; Full-Body Hybrid Complex; Progressive Complex (increasing reps each round)

common_mistakes: Inconsistent work intervals; Insufficient intensity; Excessive rest; Choosing inappropriate exercises; Poor pacing strategy; Improper form due to time constraints; Inadequate recovery between sessions; Movement selection incompatibility.
description: The Tabata protocol is a specific interval training format consisting of 20 seconds of maximum effort followed by 10 seconds of rest, repeated for 8 rounds (4 minutes total), creating one of the most time-efficient and intense HIIT modalities for maximizing cardiovascular and metabolic adaptations.
equipment: Timer (with Tabata programming), Various Equipment Depending on Selected Exercises, Non-slip Surface
form_tips: Select appropriate exercises for 20-second intervals; Maintain maximum sustainable intensity; Use full 10-second rest periods; Choose compound movements for maximum effectiveness; Scale exercises to fitness level; Focus on quality movement despite fatigue; Breathe properly despite high intensity; Rest adequately between different Tabata sets.
instructions:
1. Select appropriate exercise(s) for Tabata format (e.g., bodyweight squats, kettlebell swings, mountain climbers).
2. Set timer for 20-second work intervals and 10-second rest intervals, 8 rounds total.
3. Begin first work interval with maximum sustainable intensity.
4. Perform as many quality repetitions as possible within 20-second work period.
5. Rest completely during 10-second recovery interval.
6. Resume maximum effort at start of next 20-second work period.
7. Complete all 8 rounds (4 minutes total) of selected exercise.
8. For full Tabata workout, rest 1-2 minutes, then repeat protocol with different exercise for 2-4 total Tabata sets.
name: Tabata Protocol
variations: Single-Exercise Tabata; Multi-Exercise Tabata (different movement each round); Alternating Exercise Tabata (two exercises); Upper/Lower Tabata; Progressive Tabata (increasing difficulty each round); Regression Tabata (decreasing difficulty each round)

common_mistakes: Rotating from low back; Poor row technique; Limited core engagement; Unstable suspension point; Excessive body rotation; Losing tension in straps; Improper foot position; Inconsistent tempo.
description: The TRX rotational row combines upper body pulling strength with core stability and rotational movement, creating an effective HIIT exercise that builds functional strength through multiple planes of motion while elevating heart rate through continuous controlled movement.
equipment: TRX Suspension Trainer (or similar), Anchor Point, Non-slip Surface
form_tips: Maintain tension in straps throughout; Generate rotation from core, not arms; Keep shoulders down and back; Control body position during entire movement; Engage core continuously; Use full range of motion during row; Maintain consistent speed during rotation; Position feet appropriately for difficulty level.
instructions:
1. Adjust TRX straps to mid-length position.
2. Stand facing anchor point, grasp handles with palms facing each other.
3. Walk feet forward to create approximately 45-degree angle with floor, arms fully extended.
4. Engage core and establish proper alignment: straight line from head to heels.
5. Pull body toward handles by retracting shoulder blades and bending elbows.
6. At top of row, rotate torso to right side while maintaining tension in straps.
7. Return to center position with controlled movement.
8. Lower body back to starting position, arms extended, then repeat with rotation to left side.
name: TRX Rotational Row
variations: Single-Arm Rotational Row; Power Rotational Row (faster tempo); TRX Row with Longer Hold; Curtsy Lunge to Rotational Row; Feet Elevated Rotational Row; TRX Complex: Row to Rotation to Press

common_mistakes: Excessive lower back stress; Poor hip activation; Limited range during swings; Insufficient weight transfer; Keeping kettlebell too low; Poor breathing coordination; Improper foot placement during lunge; Rushing transitions.
description: The kettlebell swing to reverse lunge combines dynamic hip hinging with unilateral leg work, creating a challenging HIIT exercise that develops posterior chain power, cardiovascular fitness, and single-leg stability in one comprehensive movement pattern.
equipment: Kettlebell (12-24kg), Non-slip Surface
form_tips: Generate swing power from hips, not arms; Maintain neutral spine during swing; Fully extend hips at top of swing; Step back far enough for proper lunge alignment; Keep front knee aligned with toes; Control kettlebell during transition phases; Breathe rhythmically with movement (exhale during swing); Engage core throughout entire sequence.
instructions:
1. Begin standing with feet shoulder-width apart, kettlebell on floor between feet.
2. Hinge at hips to grasp kettlebell with both hands, maintaining flat back.
3. Hike kettlebell back between legs, creating tension in hamstrings and glutes.
4. Explosively drive hips forward, straightening legs to propel kettlebell forward and up.
5. Allow kettlebell to rise to chest height through arm extension.
6. As kettlebell begins to descend, step back with right leg into reverse lunge position.
7. Lower body until both knees form approximately 90-degree angles.
8. Push through left heel to return to standing, then immediately begin next swing, alternating lunge leg with each repetition.
name: Kettlebell Swing to Reverse Lunge
variations: Single-Arm Swing to Lunge; Swing to Lateral Lunge; Swing to Curtsy Lunge; Heavy Swing to Bodyweight Lunge; American Swing (overhead) to Lunge; Swing to Lunge with Rotation

common_mistakes: Limited crossover range; Poor core engagement; Looking down; Placing hands too far forward; Excessive hip rotation; Incomplete knee drive; Uneven rhythm; Inconsistent breathing pattern.
description: The mountain climber crossover adds rotational elements to traditional mountain climbers, creating a more challenging HIIT exercise that develops core strength, shoulder stability, and hip mobility while significantly elevating heart rate through continuous dynamic movement.
equipment: Non-slip Surface, Optional: Exercise Mat, Sliders (for increased difficulty)
form_tips: Maintain rigid plank position throughout; Keep shoulders stacked directly over wrists; Drive knees toward opposite elbows with control; Engage core continuously; Maintain consistent rhythm despite fatigue; Keep hips level during crossover motion; Breathe rhythmically with movement; Focus on quality of movement over speed.
instructions:
1. Begin in high plank position with hands directly under shoulders, body forming straight line.
2. Engage core to maintain stable upper body and prevent hip rotation.
3. Drive right knee forward and across body toward left elbow.
4. Return right foot to starting position with control.
5. Drive left knee forward and across body toward right elbow.
6. Continue alternating legs in fluid, controlled motion.
7. Maintain consistent pace and breathing pattern throughout set.
8. Focus on rotation coming from hips and core rather than shifting shoulders.
name: Mountain Climber Crossover
variations: Slow Controlled Crossover; Slider Mountain Climber Crossover; Mountain Climber Complex (standard + crossover); Elevated Crossover (feet on bench); Cross-Body Mountain Climber with Push-Up; Weighted Vest Crossover

common_mistakes: Dropping kettlebell directly down; Using too much shoulder rotation; Rounding lower back; Incomplete row depth; Limited lunging range; Poor breathing timing; Lacking core engagement; Uneven weight distribution.
description: The dumbbell reverse lunge to row combines lower body stability with upper body pulling strength in one comprehensive exercise, creating an effective HIIT movement that builds functional strength, coordination, and metabolic capacity through multi-joint patterning.
equipment: Dumbbells (Pair), Non-slip Surface
form_tips: Maintain upright torso during lunge; Step back far enough for proper alignment; Keep front knee tracking over foot; Pull shoulder blades together during row; Engage core throughout movement; Breathe rhythmically with exertion; Control transitions between phases; Maintain consistent tempo across repetitions.
instructions:
1. Begin standing with feet hip-width apart, holding dumbbells at sides.
2. Step backward with right leg into reverse lunge position.
3. Lower body until both knees form approximately 90-degree angles.
4. While maintaining lunge position, hinge forward slightly at hips with flat back.
5. Perform rowing movement by pulling dumbbells toward lower ribs, keeping elbows close to body.
6. Squeeze shoulder blades together at top of row.
7. Lower dumbbells with control, then drive through left heel to return to standing.
8. Repeat sequence, alternating lunge leg with each repetition.
name: Dumbbell Reverse Lunge to Row
variations: Single-Arm Lunge Row (alternating); Curtsy Lunge to Row; Forward Lunge to Row; Tempo Lunge Row (slowing phases); Heavy-Light Contrast (one heavy, one light dumbbell); Lunge Row to Bicep Curl

common_mistakes: Rushing transitions; Improper Turkish get-up form; Limited mobility for get-up; Incomplete burpee movement; Poor alignment during get-up; Not scaling appropriately; Inconsistent breathing; Using momentum rather than control.
description: The burpee to Turkish get-up contrasts explosive movement with deliberate, technical work, creating a comprehensive HIIT exercise that develops power, mobility, and strength while building mental focus through contrasting movement patterns.
equipment: Kettlebell or Dumbbell (Light to Moderate), Non-slip Surface, Exercise Mat
form_tips: Master each movement separately before combining; Scale weight appropriately (start lighter); Control transitions between movements; Maintain proper alignment during get-up; Perform full range of motion for burpee; Focus on quality over speed, especially during get-up; Breathe rhythmically despite contrasting tempos; Reset between repetitions if needed.
instructions:
1. Begin standing with feet shoulder-width apart, kettlebell or dumbbell on floor to side.
2. Perform standard burpee: squat down, place hands on floor, jump feet back to plank position.
3. Perform push-up, then jump feet forward toward hands, returning to squat position.
4. Jump up explosively, then immediately lie on back beside weight.
5. Grasp kettlebell/dumbbell in right hand, pressing it overhead with arm vertical.
6. Execute Turkish get-up by sequentially: rolling to elbow, sweeping leg through, rising to knee, standing up.
7. Reverse get-up movement to return safely to floor, keeping weight overhead throughout.
8. Return weight to ground, then repeat entire sequence, alternating get-up side each repetition.
name: Burpee to Turkish Get-Up
variations: Half Get-Up to Burpee; Burpee to Single-Leg Stand (simplified); Weighted Burpee to Get-Up; Turkish Sit-Up to Burpee (modified version); Time-Under-Tension Get-Up Complex; Burpee to Windmill (alternative skilled movement)

common_mistakes: Inconsistent transitions; Insufficient recovery between stations; Improper rowing form; Poor spinning technique; Losing intensity through transitions; Pacing too aggressively; Neglecting proper setup adjustments; Improper breathing patterns.
description: The rower to assault bike interval combines two high-output cardio modalities into an intense circuit format, creating a comprehensive HIIT workout that maximizes caloric expenditure, cardiovascular challenge, and full-body conditioning through complementary movement patterns.
equipment: Rowing Machine, Assault/Air Bike, Timer, Optional: Heart Rate Monitor
form_tips: Adjust equipment properly before starting; Maintain proper technique on both machines; Transition efficiently between stations; Use consistent pacing strategy appropriate for interval length; Focus on power output during work intervals; Use complete recovery during rest periods; Breathe rhythmically despite high exertion; Scale intensity based on fitness level.
instructions:
1. Set up both machines in close proximity to minimize transition time.
2. Begin with proper adjustments to both rowing machine and assault bike.
3. Start on rower, warming up for 2-3 minutes at moderate pace.
4. Perform high-intensity interval on rower for prescribed time (typically 30-60 seconds at 85-95% effort).
5. Transition quickly but safely to assault bike.
6. Complete high-intensity interval on assault bike for equal time as rowing interval.
7. Rest completely for prescribed recovery period (typically 1:1 or 2:1 work-to-rest ratio).
8. Repeat circuit for prescribed number of rounds (typically 4-8 total), focusing on maintaining power output across all intervals.
name: Rower to Assault Bike Interval
variations: Triple Threat (Row/Bike/Ski Erg); Pyramid Intervals (increasing/decreasing work periods); Calorie-Based Intervals; Heart Rate Recovery Intervals; EMOM Machine Circuit; Sprint/Endurance Contrast (alternating short/long intervals)
